title: When a friend dies
subtitle: The particular shape of grief for a peer
excerpt: Losing a friend in mid-life is a different shape of grief from losing a parent. The loss arrives without institutional scaffolding, with complicated feelings about age and mortality, and without clear social permission to be undone by it. This reading names what makes it distinctive.
sourceNotes: Synthesised from grief research and literature on peer loss and mid-life bereavement. No single source. Framing original to homemade.education.

practice.whenToUse: When grieving the loss of a friend and the experience feels complicated, unsupported, or unexpectedly large

content.text: When a parent dies, there is a social scaffolding for it. People understand, there is leave, there is a category in the world. When a friend dies, the scaffolding is thinner. The grief is real and sometimes very large, but the infrastructure around it is not built for it.
The particular features of friend loss

Losing a friend in mid-life carries features that the loss of an older relative doesn't. The friend is close to you in age. Their death makes age and mortality visible in a different way. It arrives in the body as information about your own lifespan, not just as the loss of a person.

Friends hold specific history: particular years, private jokes, the version of you that existed in that friendship. When the friend dies, that version of you loses its witness. The grief includes losing the person who knew you in that specific way.

People who lose friends sometimes report feeling that their grief is not fully sanctioned. Workplaces don't give bereavement leave for friends in most contexts. The friend's family, not you, is the named griever. Other people may assume you are less affected than a family member would be.

This can produce a doubling of the grief: the loss itself, and the isolation of grieving without recognition. Both are real. The grief you are feeling does not need to be proportional to your formal relationship to the person.

Naming the loss specifically. Not "I've had a bereavement" but "my friend died, and we'd known each other since we were twenty-three, and I miss her specifically." The specificity resists the minimising.
Finding others who knew the friend, if possible. People who share the same stories and the same memories of the actual person.
Drawn from grief research on peer loss and mid-life bereavement. No single source. Framing by homemade.education.
